Metadata.Coram: Judith Prakash JA, Steven Chong JA and Woo Bih Li J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Jason Aleksander Kardachi

Facts.Text: Jason Aleksander Kardachi is a restructuring and insolvency professional based in Singapore who is frequently appointed as a director of distressed or insolvent companies.
On 6 September 2010, Jason Aleksander Kardachi was appointed as the Managing Director of Borrelli Walsh Pte Ltd.

On 8 November 2010, Jason Aleksander Kardachi was appointed as a director of four companies in the Global Brands Group: Global Brands F&B Pte Ltd, Consolidated Brands (Asia) Pte Ltd, Global Brands Retail Pte Ltd, and Global Brands Holdings Pte Ltd.
The four Global Brands companies were insolvent and had ceased business operations prior to the appointment of Jason Aleksander Kardachi as a director.
Section 155A of the Companies Act, concerning the disqualification of directors in relation to multiple struck-off companies, came into effect.
On 18 April 2016, Jason Aleksander Kardachi initiated internal instructions to strike off Consolidated Brands (Asia) Pte Ltd.

On 27 June 2016, Jason Aleksander Kardachi requested a third-party creditor to release securities over the assets of Global Brands Retail Pte Ltd and Global Brands Holdings Pte Ltd to facilitate their strike-off applications.
On 29 July 2016, the regulatory authority issued a notice regarding the intention to strike off Global Brands F&B Pte Ltd, stating no action was needed if there was no objection.
On 7 November 2016, Global Brands F&B Pte Ltd was struck off the register.
On 27 September 2017, the regulatory authority issued a statutory notice regarding the impending strike-off of Consolidated Brands (Asia) Pte Ltd.
On 8 January 2018, both Consolidated Brands (Asia) Pte Ltd and Global Brands Retail Pte Ltd were struck off the register.
On 6 August 2018, Global Brands Holdings Pte Ltd was struck off the register.
On 31 August 2018, the regulatory authority issued a letter stating that Jason Aleksander Kardachi was disqualified from acting as a director for five years, effective from 8 January 2018.
By October 2018, Jason Aleksander Kardachi held directorships in at least 123 Singapore-incorporated companies.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director’s disqualification is triggered immediately upon the striking off of a third company under the five-year statutory period, and whether a professional director can obtain a blanket exemption to continue managing other corporate entities despite the disqualification.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Section 155A(1) of the Companies Act, a person who was a director of a company at the time it was struck off, and who was also a director of at least two other companies struck off within the preceding five years, is disqualified for five years from the date the last company was struck off. Section 155A(3) allows the court to grant leave for a person to act as a director during this period if there are sufficient exculpatory reasons or circumstances justifying the exception.
Party_Details.Application: Jason Aleksander Kardachi served as a director for four interrelated companies that were all struck off within a two-year period. He asserted that the statutory disqualification was either not triggered or should be mitigated because he was a professional insolvency practitioner tasked with winding down these specific entities. He argued that the delays in striking off were due to complex security releases and misleading regulatory correspondence. He sought a blanket order allowing him to maintain his 120+ other directorships to prevent professional hardship.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the ordinary meaning of the statute requires three companies to be struck off within a five-year period *before* the date of the final striking-off event. Consequently, the disqualification was not triggered by the second and third events in January 2018, but rather by the fourth event in August 2018. However, on the merits of the leave application, the court found no exculpatory reasons for the repeated failure to comply with statutory filing obligations. The director's professional status as an insolvency practitioner did not entitle him to more lenient treatment; rather, such professionals are expected to maintain strict compliance with corporate regulations. The request for a blanket exemption was denied because the statute requires leave to be sought for specific companies, and allowing a professional director to ignore a disqualification order would undermine the protective purpose of the legislation.

Metadata.Coram: Judith Prakash JA, Steven Chong JA and Woo Bih Li J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Attorney-General

Facts.Text: The Attorney-General represents the Minister for Finance and the regulatory interests of the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority.
The regulatory authority issued a formal notification of disqualification to a director who had been associated with four companies struck off the register within a five-year span.

The Attorney-General opposed the application for a blanket exemption from the disqualification order, asserting that the director failed to provide exculpatory evidence for the persistent filing defaults.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the state's interest in maintaining corporate regulatory standards precludes granting a broad exemption from director disqualification to a professional insolvency practitioner.
Party_Details.Rule: The statutory disqualification regime is intended to protect the public and the corporate environment from directors who demonstrate a consistent failure to manage companies according to the law. Leave to bypass a disqualification must be narrow in scope and supported by evidence that the underlying defaults were unavoidable or that the director's continued service is essential for specific entities.
Party_Details.Application: The Attorney-General maintained that the director’s history of directorships in multiple companies that were struck off due to filing defaults triggered a mandatory five-year disqualification. The state argued that allowing a professional director to obtain a general waiver would negate the deterrent effect of the statute and that the director’s excuse—that he was busy restructuring the entities—did not relieve him of the obligation to ensure basic statutory compliance.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The state's position regarding the necessity of the disqualification was largely upheld. The court agreed that a professional director remains fully responsible for compliance and that the administrative burden of restructuring does not excuse a total failure to file annual returns. While the court adjusted the commencement date of the disqualification based on a technical reading of the timeline, it affirmed that a blanket leave application seeking to act for any company is legally unsupported. The opposition to the leave application was successful because the director failed to demonstrate any exceptional circumstances or exculpatory reasons for the repeated striking-off events.
